perturbation.original_text: The text of Section 2. A. and B. of the Agreement is hereby deleted in its entirety and the following language is inserted in its stead: [***]
perturbation.changed_text: The text of Section 2. A. and B. of the Agreement is hereby deleted in its entirety.
perturbation.explanation: Omitting the new language '[***]' which replaces Section 2. A. and B removes essential contract terms. If Section 2. A. and B contained clauses that protect consumers, such as cancellation or return policies, their complete removal without replacement could violate consumer protection laws, depending on the content of the original sections and applicable state laws (e.g. Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act). The replaced section is now ambiguous, giving the company liberty to act outside regulations or requirements of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Consumer Fraud Act prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of trade or commerce. If the original Section 2 A and B contained provisions beneficial to consumers (e.g., concerning service levels, guarantees or rights related to the co-branded product), deleting them entirely without replacement, especially if the replacement language was meant to clarify or enhance consumer rights, could be construed as a deceptive practice if it degrades consumer protections or misrepresents the benefits of the co-branding arrangement. Failing to specify the replacement terms leads to a violation of disclosing material information to the consumer.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes Back to Act Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Printer-Friendly Version Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. ( ) Top Home | Legislation & Laws | House | Senate | My Legislation | Disclaimers | Email This site is maintained for the Illinois General Assembly by the Legislative Information System, 705 Stratton Building, Springfield, Illinois 62706 Contact ILGA Webmaster
perturbation.justification: The original text deletes Section 2. A. and B. and replaces it with new language. The changed text deletes Section 2. A. and B. entirely, omitting the replacement language. This omission removes potentially important consumer protections without replacement. The Illinois Consumer Fraud Act, as described in the Law Explanation, prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices. If Section 2. A. and B. contained consumer-beneficial provisions, deleting them without replacement could be considered a deceptive practice. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, where essential information is removed, potentially creating legal loopholes and non-compliance, specifically with consumer protection laws, that could make the contract non-compliant. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, as it references the Illinois Compiled Statutes, implying state-level consumer protection laws could be implicated.

perturbation.original_text: Except as modified hereby, the Agreement and its terms and provisions are hereby ratified and confirmed for all purposes and in all respects.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement and its terms and provisions are hereby ratified and confirmed for all purposes and in all respects.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'Except as modified hereby' eliminates the explicit acknowledgment that the original agreement has been changed. This omission could create legal ambiguity about which version of the agreement is currently in effect. If the modifications significantly alter consumer rights established in the original agreement, failing to acknowledge those changes explicitly may violate disclosure requirements under the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act, potentially misleading consumers about their rights and obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the 'Except as modified hereby' clause, the modified text suggests the original agreement is fully in force, without clearly indicating any changes. This is a deceptive act because it fails to clearly and conspicuously disclose material modifications to the agreement that could impact consumer rights or obligations, specifically if the amendment changed some consumer rights to the detriment of the consumer.

Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. perturbation.justification: The modified text, by omitting "Except as modified hereby," creates a contradiction by implying that the original agreement remains fully in effect despite any modifications made. The Law Explanation suggests this omission is deceptive because it doesn't clearly disclose changes impacting consumer rights, especially if amendments diminish those rights. The original text explicitly acknowledges the agreement has been changed, so removing the phrase makes the contract contradict its new form because it suggests the original and modified agreement are the same. The Illinois Compiled Statutes snippet reinforces the need for up-to-date legal information, stating "Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law." This omission could mislead consumers by presenting an outdated or inaccurate view of their rights, thus contradicting consumer protection principles by not clearly disclosing material modifications.
